threats.title: initializeAlphaIndices Access Control Mismatch Permanently Blocks Generation Increment
threats.hypothesis_description: EntropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices() uses onlyOwner modifier, but TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls it. Since TraitForgeNft is set as allowedCaller (not owner), the call reverts. After 10,000 gen-1 tokens are minted, all subsequent minting permanently halts because _mintInternal triggers _incrementGeneration which reverts on the unauthorized call to initializeAlphaIndices.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic protocol-breaking bug. The deployment script sets TraitForgeNft as allowedCaller of EntropyGenerator but never transfers ownership. The modifier mismatch (onlyOwner vs onlyAllowedCaller) guarantees reversion on generation increment.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script (deployTasks.ts) deploys EntropyGenerator with nft as constructor arg (sets allowedCaller), but never calls entropyGenerator.transferOwnership(nft). Owner remains deployer EOA.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initializeAlphaIndices has `onlyOwner` modifier but is called by TraitForgeNft (which is `allowedCaller`, not owner)

preconditions: generationMintCounts[currentGeneration] reaches maxTokensPerGen (10000)
TraitForgeNft is not the owner of EntropyGenerator

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Cross-contract call with mismatched access control modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _incrementGeneration calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices() but initializeAlphaIndices requires onlyOwner and TraitForgeNft is allowedCaller not owner
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use onlyAllowedCaller or combined modifier; deployment should transfer ownership

analysis_questions: Does any deployment path transfer EntropyGenerator ownership to TraitForgeNft?
Can the owner call initializeAlphaIndices separately to unblock the system?
Is there any alternate generation increment path that avoids this call?
validation_requirements: Confirm _incrementGeneration reverts when called by TraitForgeNft
Verify no ownership transfer in deployment scripts
Test that gen 1 mint cap triggers the revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker action needed. After 10,000 gen-1 tokens are minted, the 10,001st mint triggers _incrementGeneration which calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(). This reverts because msg.sender is TraitForgeNft (not owner). All minting across the entire protocol permanently halts.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol-breaking: no new tokens can ever be minted after gen 1 fills, destroying all future minting revenue and game progression
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users - entire minting system frozen
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner could manually call initializeAlphaIndices() then generation state would need manual correction, but _incrementGeneration would still fail on next gen transition

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for generation transition crossing gen boundary

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately observable when generation limit reached

threats.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Non-Functional After Generation 1
threats.hypothesis_description: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as its while-loop condition. _tokenIds is a global monotonically increasing counter, while maxTokensPerGen is 10,000. After generation 1 completes (10,000 tokens), _tokenIds >= 10,000 and the loop never executes. mintWithBudget becomes permanently non-functional for all subsequent generations, refunding all ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop condition compares a global counter (_tokenIds) against a per-generation limit (maxTokensPerGen), which is a clear logic error. The condition should use generationMintCounts[currentGeneration].

proof_of_signal.pattern: while (budgetLeft >= mintPrice && _tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen) uses global _tokenIds instead of generationMintCounts[currentGeneration]

preconditions: _tokenIds >= maxTokensPerGen (10,000 total tokens minted across all generations)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Global counter compared to per-generation limit
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _tokenIds is never reset between generations; maxTokensPerGen = 10000

analysis_questions: Is _tokenIds ever reset or decremented?
Does any other path compensate for this bug?
What percentage of users would use mintWithBudget vs mintToken?
validation_requirements: Confirm _tokenIds >= 10000 after gen 1
Test mintWithBudget in gen 2+ returns all ETH as refund
Verify the loop body never executes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker action needed. After gen 1 fills, any user calling mintWithBudget has their entire msg.value refunded because the while loop condition (_tokenIds < 10000) is always false. Batch minting is permanently disabled for gen 2+.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of batch minting functionality; users must mint one at a time via mintToken, degrading UX significantly
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users trying to batch-mint in generations 2-10

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for mintWithBudget in generation 2+

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable immediately when users try batch minting in gen 2

threats.title: Forger Off-by-One Allows One Extra Forge Beyond Intended Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` (inclusive), allowing listing when count equals potential. forgeWithListed then increments count without rechecking. A forger with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times: the last listing passes at count=N (N<=N is true), then forge increments to N+1.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comparison operator should be strict less-than (<) not less-than-or-equal (<=). This off-by-one affects every forger token, allowing one extra forge each. Additionally, forgeWithListed never rechecks the forger's forge potential after listing, compounding the issue when yearly resets occur.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential uses <= instead of <; forgeWithListed line 121 increments without recheck

preconditions: Forger token with forgePotential > 0
Forger forgingCounts equal to forgePotential

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Off-by-one: <= instead of < in forge potential check; no recheck at forge time
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: forgingCounts can reach forgePotential+1 for any forger
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing forge potential recheck in forgeWithListed; wrong comparison operator in listForForging

analysis_questions: Trace exact forgingCounts progression for forgePotential=1
Does the yearly reset compound this issue further?
How does the merger check compare (it uses post-increment <= which is correct)?
validation_requirements: Unit test: forge with potential=1 should allow exactly 1 forge, verify it allows 2
Test across year boundary: list, wait 1 year, forge resets, re-forge

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any forger owner with forgePotential=N can forge N+1 times. With forgePotential=1: list at count=0 (0<=1 passes), forge->count=1; list at count=1 (1<=1 passes), forge->count=2. Two forges with potential=1. Each extra forge produces an additional NFT entity and earns the forger an extra fee.
prerequisites: Merger willing to pay forging fee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Every forger earns one extra forging fee and produces one extra entity, inflating supply by ~10% (each forgePotential effectively +1)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All forger token holders benefit; dilutes value for all holders

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary test for forge count at exactly forgePotential

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be detected by monitoring forgingCounts vs forgePotential

threats.title: Entropy Slot Recycling Makes All Post-Gen-1 Token Traits Predictable and Repeated
threats.hypothesis_description: EntropyGenerator stores 770 slots × 13 values = 10,010 entropy values. getNextEntropy wraps currentSlotIndex back to 0 after reaching slot 769. With 10,000 tokens per generation and up to 10 generations (100,000 tokens), entropy values repeat cyclically after the first 10,010 mints. Tokens in later generations have identical entropy to gen-1 tokens, fully predictable by any observer.
threats.investigation_rationale: Entropy slots are never regenerated across generations. _incrementGeneration only re-randomizes the alpha index (slotIndexSelectionPoint/numberIndexSelectionPoint) but does not rewrite entropySlots. After wraparound, the same 6-digit values repeat in the same order.

proof_of_signal.pattern: currentSlotIndex wraps to 0 when >= maxSlotIndex-1; entropySlots never rewritten between generations

preconditions: More than 10,010 tokens minted across all generations
Entropy batches only written once

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Slot index wraps to 0 with no re-randomization; writeEntropyBatch guards prevent rewriting
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Tokens 10,011+ have identical entropy to tokens 1-10,010
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No entropy regeneration mechanism between generations

analysis_questions: After gen-1 mints + forged entities, how quickly does wraparound occur?
Does the alpha index change compensate for recycled entropy?
Can forged entities push the index past 10,010 within gen 1?
validation_requirements: Trace getNextEntropy index progression through gen transitions
Verify entropy repetition after 10,010 calls
Confirm writeEntropyBatch cannot be called again

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker who observed gen-1 entropy values knows exactly what entropy gen-2+ tokens will receive. They can time mints to capture high-value entropies (high nuke factor, high forge potential) in every generation, extracting maximum NukeFund value repeatedly.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of entropy fairness in later generations; attacker can monopolize high-value tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All gen-2+ minters competing against informed attackers

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for entropy wraparound behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect - appears as normal minting

threats.title: Excess ETH Permanently Locked in EntityForging Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed checks `msg.value >= forgingFee` but only distributes exactly forgingFee (split between nukeFund and forger owner). Any excess ETH (msg.value - forgingFee) remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract with no withdrawal mechanism, receive function, or sweep function.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike mintToken which refunds excess, forgeWithListed keeps surplus. EntityForging has no fallback for trapped ETH recovery. Over time, accumulated excess ETH becomes permanently irrecoverable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) with no refund of (msg.value - forgingFee); devFee and forgerShare sum to forgingFee, not msg.value

preconditions: User sends msg.value > forgingFee to forgeWithListed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Overpayment accepted but not refunded; no withdrawal function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: devFee = forgingFee/taxCut, forgerShare = forgingFee-devFee; excess = msg.value-forgingFee is trapped
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund logic, no sweep function, no require(msg.value == forgingFee)

analysis_questions: Is there any receive() or fallback() that could extract trapped ETH?
Can owner withdraw trapped ETH through any mechanism?
How much ETH could accumulate over time?
validation_requirements: Test forgeWithListed with msg.value > forgingFee
Verify contract balance increases by excess amount
Confirm no withdrawal mechanism exists

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users accidentally overpaying forging fees lose the excess permanently. Over time, ETH accumulates in the EntityForging contract with no recovery mechanism.
prerequisites: User sends more ETH than the forging fee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct loss of user ETH on every overpayment; cumulative loss over protocol lifetime
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who overpays forging fees

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for overpayment scenario in forgeWithListed

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via contract balance vs expected balance

threats.title: Stale listedTokenIds After Cancellation Causes Incorrect Forging Listing Behavior
threats.hypothesis_description: _cancelListingForForging deletes listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] but does NOT clear listedTokenIds[tokenId]. This stale pointer causes issues: (1) _beforeTokenTransfer queries getListedTokenIds which returns non-zero for cancelled tokens, triggering unnecessary listing checks; (2) if a new listing reuses the same listing index, the stale pointer could cause cross-token interference.
threats.investigation_rationale: The listing and token-to-listing mappings are inconsistent after cancellation. While listForForging creates a new entry (incrementing listingCount), the old stale pointer remains. In _beforeTokenTransfer, getListedTokenIds returns the stale value, and the subsequent check may interact with a different token's listing data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] without delete listedTokenIds[tokenId]; stale mapping persists

preconditions: Token was listed and then cancelled or forged
Token is subsequently transferred

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incomplete state cleanup: listings deleted but listedTokenIds not cleared
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: listedTokenIds[tokenId] returns stale non-zero value after cancellation, affecting _beforeTokenTransfer logic

analysis_questions: Can the stale pointer cause a different token's listing to be cancelled?
Does listingCount ever get reused?
What happens in _beforeTokenTransfer when listing data is zeroed but listedId > 0?
validation_requirements: Test: cancel listing, then transfer token, verify _beforeTokenTransfer behavior
Test: cancel listing for token A, then list token B at same listingCount slot
Verify if cross-token cancellation is possible

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token A listed at index 5, then cancelled. listedTokenIds[A] still = 5. listings[5] is deleted (zeroed). Now token B is listed at index 6. When token A is transferred, _beforeTokenTransfer checks listedTokenIds[A] = 5, reads zeroed listings[5], and the listing.tokenId/account/isListed checks fail so no cancellation occurs. This is relatively benign. But if listing index recycling occurs, it could cause unintended cancellation of another token's listing.
prerequisites: Token listing followed by cancellation/forge, then transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential for incorrect listing state; minor gas waste on unnecessary checks; possible cross-listing interference if indices collide
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who list, cancel, and re-list tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Re-listing would overwrite the stale pointer

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for state consistency after listing cancellation

threats.title: Generation Counter Can Exceed maxGeneration Via _incrementGeneration
threats.hypothesis_description: _incrementGeneration does not check whether currentGeneration < maxGeneration before incrementing. If generation 10 (maxGeneration) fills up, _incrementGeneration would set currentGeneration to 11, which exceeds the intended maximum. Subsequent mints would occur in an out-of-bounds generation.
threats.investigation_rationale: While forge() checks `newGeneration <= maxGeneration`, _incrementGeneration (triggered by minting) has no such guard. This violates the stated game design of max 10 generations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _incrementGeneration increments currentGeneration without checking against maxGeneration

preconditions: currentGeneration == maxGeneration
generationMintCounts[maxGeneration] reaches maxTokensPerGen
initializeAlphaIndices issue is resolved (otherwise gen increment reverts anyway)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Protocol allows minting beyond intended max generation

analysis_questions: Does any calling path prevent this from being reached?
What properties do gen-11 tokens have?
Does this affect forge() which checks newGeneration <= maxGeneration?
validation_requirements: Test _incrementGeneration when currentGeneration == maxGeneration
Verify currentGeneration becomes 11
Check impact on pricing and entropy

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the initializeAlphaIndices issue is resolved, after generation 10 fills up, _incrementGeneration sets currentGeneration=11. Tokens minted in gen 11 exist but shouldn't. These tokens can't be forged (forge checks maxGeneration), creating asymmetric token properties.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Tokens minted beyond maxGeneration; violation of stated game rules; potential pricing anomalies
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users minting tokens in generation > maxGeneration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires setMaxGeneration update or contract fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for generation boundary behavior at maxGeneration

threats.title: NukeFund receive() DoS via ETH Transfer Failure to devAddress/daoAddress
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.receive() sends devShare ETH to one of three addresses (devAddress, owner(), daoAddress) based on airdrop state. Each transfer uses a require(success) check. If the recipient contract rejects ETH (e.g., no receive/fallback or gas-intensive callback), the entire receive() reverts. Since ALL minting revenue flows through NukeFund.receive(), this would permanently brick minting, trading, and forging.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment passes DevFund contract as devAddress. DevFund.receive() exists but sends remainder to owner(). If owner() of DevFund is a contract without receive(), DevFund.receive() reverts, causing NukeFund.receive() to revert, cascading to block all ETH-generating protocol operations.
deployment_assumptions: devAddress, daoAddress, and owner() must all be capable of receiving ETH without reverting
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts passes devFund.getAddress() as _devAddress for NukeFund. DevFund.receive() sends dust to owner(). If owner rejects ETH, chain of reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Three conditional branches all use require(success) after call{value: devShare}; failure in any branch reverts the entire receive()

preconditions: One of devAddress, owner(), or daoAddress cannot receive ETH
The corresponding airdrop state branch is active

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Required ETH transfers to external addresses in receive(); any failure reverts entire function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All protocol ETH flows through this receive(); single point of failure
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try-catch, no escrow pattern, no fallback if transfer fails

analysis_questions: Can DevFund.receive() revert under any conditions?
What happens if owner() of DevFund is a multisig with high gas requirements?
Is daoAddress (DAOFund) guaranteed to accept ETH?
validation_requirements: Test NukeFund.receive() when devAddress is a reverting contract
Test full mint flow when devShare transfer fails
Verify cascading revert blocks all minting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If DevFund.receive() reverts (e.g., owner() of DevFund is changed to a contract that rejects ETH, or totalDevWeight causes dust to be sent to reverting owner), all calls to NukeFund.receive() revert. This blocks _distributeFunds in TraitForgeNft, halting all minting. EntityTrading.buyNFT and EntityForging.forgeWithListed also fail when sending to NukeFund.
prerequisites: One recipient address in the devShare chain rejects ETH

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol halt: no minting, trading, or forging possible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can change devAddress/daoAddress to fix, but requires identifying the issue

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for NukeFund.receive() with reverting recipients

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas limits on ETH transfers to contracts
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time a recipient becomes non-receivable

threats.title: Forging listing cancellation can permanently brick ALL NFT transfers if EntityForging is unset or miswired
threats.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft._beforeTokenTransfer() unconditionally calls into entityForgingContract (getListedTokenIds / getListings) for every transfer. If entityForgingContract is the zero address or a non-contract address, these external calls will revert, bricking transfers/mints/burns that rely on the hook.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a hard external dependency in the ERC721 transfer hook. Because _beforeTokenTransfer is executed on mint, burn, and transfer, a single misconfiguration can halt the entire NFT lifecycle and downstream systems (trading escrow, nuking burns, forging).
deployment_assumptions: TraitForgeNft.entityForgingContract is not set before any mint/transfer/burn occurs, OR it is set to an EOA/non-contract, OR it is later updated to an incompatible contract
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts wires entityForgingContract via nft.setEntityForgingContract(entityForging.getAddress()), but there is no on-chain safeguard in TraitForgeNft preventing operation before wiring or after misconfiguration.

inventory_refs.function: forge(address newOwner, uint256 parent1Id, uint256 parent2Id, string memory)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _beforeTokenTransfer calls entityForgingContract.getListedTokenIds() and getListings() without checking entityForgingContract != address(0) or code length

preconditions: entityForgingContract is unset (address(0)) or misconfigured to an address without the expected interface
Any user attempts an ERC721 transfer, mint, or burn that triggers _beforeTokenTransfer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external call in ERC721 transfer hook to configurable address without address validation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A single revert in hook prevents ALL state transitions involving token movement
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `require(address(entityForgingContract)!=address(0))` or `Address.isContract` gating; no try/catch around external calls

analysis_questions: Does mint/burn/transfer revert when entityForgingContract is address(0)?
Are there any deployment-time guarantees beyond scripts (e.g., constructor wiring) that prevent this in production?
Could an owner update to an incompatible contract and accidentally brick transfers?
validation_requirements: Unit test transfer/mint/burn with entityForgingContract unset and set to EOA
Fuzz misconfiguration scenarios after some listings exist
Confirm whether ERC721Enumerable hook behavior makes this unavoidable on all transfers

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If deployment forgets to set entityForgingContract (or sets it incorrectly), any user attempting to mint, transfer, or burn will revert due to hook external call to a non-contract address, freezing the protocol’s NFT operations.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or later admin mistake (no attacker privileges required once misconfigured)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High protocol-wide disruption; nukes/trading/forging reliant on transfers/burns can be blocked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All NFT holders and all protocol users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires correct reconfiguration by owner; users cannot self-recover

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for operation when entityForgingContract is unset or misconfigured

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain symptom: all transfers/mints/burns revert with low-level call failure to entityForgingContract

threats.title: EntityTrading listings can be overwritten/relisted without clearing listedTokenIds, enabling stale-index state and unexpected behavior
threats.hypothesis_description: EntityTrading deletes listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] on buy and cancel but never clears listedTokenIds[tokenId]. This creates stale pointers similar to EntityForging. Subsequent interactions that rely on listedTokenIds (including off-chain indexers/UI, or future functions) can misinterpret listing existence, and a relist will overwrite listedTokenIds[tokenId] but listingCount continues to grow, allowing confusing states.
threats.investigation_rationale: Stale pointer patterns often become exploitable when combined with additional functionality (e.g., price updates, partial fills) or cross-contract assumptions. Even now, it can cause confusing behavior and can interact badly with TraitForgeNft’s transfer hook and external tooling.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete listings[listedTokenIds[tokenId]] without delete listedTokenIds[tokenId]

preconditions: Token is listed and then bought or cancelled
Token is later re-listed or external systems rely on listedTokenIds mapping as a source of truth

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: state-machine inconsistencies; potential future exploit surface if new functions are added that assume listedTokenIds==0 implies not listed
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cleanup of listedTokenIds[tokenId] on cancellation/sale

analysis_questions: Does any other contract read EntityTrading.listedTokenIds (currently none shown)?
Could a stale non-zero listedTokenIds cause incorrect lookups or UI-induced mis-settlement (on-chain correctness still matters)?
Are there edge cases where listedTokenIds[tokenId] points to a listing belonging to a different token after storage reuse (if listingCount resets in future versions)?

Property test: if listings[idx].isActive then listedTokenIds[tokenId]==idx and listings[idx].tokenId==tokenId

prerequisites: Token ownership and ability to list/cancel repeatedly

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate to high operational risk; potential indirect loss if future logic assumes mapping cleanliness
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract change (no upgrade path) or careful off-chain filtering

threats.test_coverage_gap: No invariant tests around mapping/listing coherence

threats.title: TraitForgeNft mint flows can be DoSed by malicious/buggy NukeFund (or unset nukeFundAddress) because funds are forwarded via raw call

threats.title: EntityTrading buyNFT can revert (and lock listed NFTs in escrow) if nukeFundAddress is unset or rejects ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: EntityTrading.buyNFT always calls transferToNukeFund which requires nukeFundAddress != 0 and requires the call succeed. If misconfigured, purchases revert. threats.title: Fully Predictable Entropy Enables Selective High-Value Token Minting for Maximum NukeFund Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: All entropy values are deterministic (derived from block.number at batch-write time), stored once, and publicly readable via getPublicEntropy(). Combined with publicly readable currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex, any user can predict exactly which entropy the next mint will yield. An attacker can calculate all 10,010 entropy values, identify the positions of the highest-value tokens (high nuke factor, 999999 alpha), and time their mints to capture them. On Base (no public mempool), the attacker simply reads on-chain state, waits for the right entropy index, and calls mintToken.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental fairness of the NFT game. The entropy determines nuke factor (ETH payout), forge potential, and role. If one player can select the best tokens while others mint blindly, the informed player systematically extracts disproportionate value from the NukeFund at the expense of all other participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPublicEntropy is public view returning getEntropy for any slot/number. currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex are private but inferable. Entire entropy sequence is deterministic from block.number at write time.

preconditions: Entropy batches have been written (writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 called)
Minting is active (not paused, whitelist passed or expired)
Attacker can read on-chain state (getPublicEntropy, slot/number indices)

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Alpha token (999999) gives initialNukeFactor=24999, claimable ~25% of fund. High-entropy tokens (e.g., 900000+) give ~22500 nukeFactor for ~22.5% of fund per nuke.

threats.title: Uninitialized ageMultiplier Nullifies Age-Based Nuke Factor Making NukeFund Exploitable Immediately
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.ageMultiplier defaults to 0 and the deployment script never calls setAgeMultplier(). calculateAge() returns (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * 0) / 365 = 0 for all tokens. This means nuke factor is purely entropy/40, completely ignoring hold time. Users can nuke tokens immediately after minimumDaysHeld (3 days) for the same payout as holding for years, undermining the core hold-to-earn mechanic.
threats.investigation_rationale: The age-based nuke factor increase is the primary economic incentive to hold tokens. With ageMultiplier=0, the economic model collapses to a simple lottery based on entropy. High-entropy token holders extract maximum value with no holding period beyond the minimum 3 days.
deployment_assumptions: setAgeMultplier is never called after deployment
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: No call to nukeFund.setAgeMultplier() anywhere in the deployment script. NukeFund.sol constructor does not set ageMultiplier.

proof_of_signal.pattern: calculateAge multiplies by ageMultiplier which is 0 by default: age = (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * ageMultiplier) / 365; ageMultiplier=0 makes entire expression 0

preconditions: ageMultiplier == 0 (default, confirmed by deployment script gap)

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token with entropy 999999: nukeFactor = 24999, claimAmount = fund * 24999/100000 ≈ 25% of fund after just 3 days

threats.title: Emergency Pause Mechanism Completely Non-Functional Across All Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: All six contracts (TraitForgeNft, EntityForging, EntityTrading, NukeFund, EntropyGenerator, DevFund) inherit OpenZeppelin Pausable and use whenNotPaused modifiers on critical functions. However, none expose external pause() or unpause() functions. OpenZeppelin's _pause() and _unpause() are internal, requiring wrapper functions. Without them, the pause mechanism is dead code—the protocol has no emergency brake. If a vulnerability is discovered in production, the owner cannot halt operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Systematic check of all six contracts confirms no public/external pause() or unpause() function exists. The whenNotPaused guards exist but can never be activated.

threats.title: Forger ForgePotential TOCTOU: Year Reset Between Listing and Forging Bypasses Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed does not re-validate the forger's forgePotential at forge time. It only increments forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] without checking against forgePotential. The check was done at listing time in listForForging, but _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called in forgeWithListed which can reset forgingCounts to 0 if a year has passed. This creates a TOCTOU: forger lists at count=forgePotential (allowed by off-by-one), a year passes, forgeWithListed resets count to 0 and increments to 1. The forger can repeat this cycle indefinitely, forging unlimited times with a single listing per year cycle.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comment in forgeWithListed says 'already checked in listForForging' but the state can change between listing and forging due to year reset.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without re-checking against forgePotential; preceded by _resetForgingCountIfNeeded which may reset count to 0

threats.title: Strategic Forging to Produce Nuke-Optimized Offspring with Predictable High Entropy
threats.hypothesis_description: Forged entity entropy is calculated as (forgerEntropy + mergerEntropy) / 2, and both parent entropies are publicly readable. An attacker can select parent pairs whose averaged entropy maximizes the child's nuke factor (initialNukeFactor = entropy/40), then nuke the offspring to extract disproportionate ETH from the NukeFund. The forging cost (minimum 0.01 ETH fee) can be far less than the nuke payout.
threats.investigation_rationale: With both parent entropies known on-chain, the child's nuke factor is fully deterministic. An attacker owning or coordinating with holders of high-entropy tokens can breed children optimized for maximum NukeFund extraction, essentially manufacturing high-value tokens at low cost.

threats.title: DevFund Integer Division Truncation Routes Small Payments Entirely to Owner
threats.hypothesis_description: DevFund.receive() calculates amountPerWeight = msg.value / totalDevWeight. When msg.value < totalDevWeight, amountPerWeight truncates to 0 and the entire msg.value is sent to the owner as 'remaining'. With deployment's totalDevWeight=300, any payment under 300 wei has amountPerWeight=0. While individual losses are tiny, systematic truncation across thousands of small transactions accumulates. The truncation also applies to the remainder on every transaction regardless of size.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code correctness defect in reward distribution. The owner systematically receives dust from every DevFund payment, and small payments are entirely redirected. Over the protocol's lifetime with many forging/trading fees, the cumulative loss to devs could be material.

preconditions: totalDevWeight > 0 (confirmed: 300 at deployment)
DevFund receives ETH payments (from NukeFund devShare)

threats.title: Airdrop Contract Failure in burn() Can Permanently Lock NukeFund ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.nuke() calls nftContract.burn(tokenId). Inside burn(), if airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false, it calls airdropContract.subUserAmount(). If this external call reverts (buggy airdrop contract, gas limits, or ownership transfer issues), the entire nuke transaction reverts. Fund balance is deducted before burn in nuke(), but since it's atomic, the revert restores it. However, if this condition persists, NO tokens can be nuked, permanently locking all NukeFund ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burn function creates a hard dependency on the airdrop contract for all nuking operations. Since the airdrop contract code is not in scope but ownership is transferred to nft, any failure in subUserAmount permanently bricks the entire nuke payout mechanism, effectively stealing all ETH in NukeFund.

proof_of_signal.pattern: burn() calls airdropContract.subUserAmount without try/catch; revert propagates to caller (nuke)

preconditions: airdropContract.airdropStarted() returns false (airdrop not yet started)
airdropContract.subUserAmount() reverts for any reason (bug, underflow, access control)
NukeFund has accumulated ETH from minting

threats.title: EntityTrading listNFTForSale Resets Nuke Maturity Timer via Transfer Hook
threats.hypothesis_description: When listing an NFT for sale via EntityTrading.listNFTForSale, the token is transferred from owner to EntityTrading contract. This triggers _beforeTokenTransfer which resets lastTokenTransferredTimestamp to block.timestamp. When the listing is cancelled (cancelListing), the token transfers back, resetting the timestamp again. Any user who lists and cancels a token loses their accumulated nuke maturity progress (minimumDaysHeld = 3 days). Buying also resets the timer for the new owner.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users approaching the 3-day nuke eligibility threshold who interact with EntityTrading unknowingly reset their timer. This creates an unexpected interaction between the trading and nuking systems that could trap users in a cycle of delayed nuke eligibility, reducing the expected economic utility of their tokens.

threats.title: NukeFund Accounting Mismatch Risk: Internal `fund` Can Diverge From Actual ETH Balance via Forced ETH or Bypassed Receive Logic
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund uses an internal `fund` variable updated only in receive(). ETH can be forced into the contract (e.g., via selfdestruct) or transferred through paths that do not execute receive() (or later EVM changes), creating a divergence between address(this).balance and fund. Attackers can also grief by forcing ETH to create misleading solvency signals or manipulating perceived APY without increasing claimable fund.

threats.title: Missing Pause/Unpause Functions Prevent Emergency Response to Active Exploits
threats.hypothesis_description: All six contracts inherit OpenZeppelin's Pausable and use whenNotPaused on critical functions, but none exposes external pause() or unpause() functions. OpenZeppelin's _pause() and _unpause() are internal functions. Without public wrappers, the pause mechanism is completely non-functional. If any vulnerability (e.g., H001, H003) is being actively exploited, the owner cannot pause the protocol to prevent further damage.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical operational security gap. The whenNotPaused modifiers create a false sense of security. In an active exploit scenario, the inability to pause allows attackers to continue extracting value while the team scrambles for a fix.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract inherits Pausable and uses whenNotPaused modifiers, but no function calls _pause() or _unpause() anywhere in any of the 6 contracts

threats.title: NukeFund Claim Cap Bypassable When potentialClaimAmount Exceeds maxAllowedClaimAmount at Default Settings
threats.hypothesis_description: The nuke() claim cap only activates when `finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam` (default 50000). The potentialClaimAmount = fund * finalNukeFactor / MAX_DENOMINATOR is used directly when finalNukeFactor <= nukeFactorMaxParam. At default settings (nukeFactorMaxParam = 50000, maxAllowedClaimDivisor = 2), this is consistent: max uncapped claim = fund * 50000 / 100000 = 50% = fund / 2. However, if the owner sets nukeFactorMaxParam higher (e.g., 80000) without adjusting maxAllowedClaimDivisor, the uncapped window expands: a token with finalNukeFactor = 79999 would claim fund * 79999 / 100000 ≈ 80% of the fund, bypassing the intended 50% cap. Additionally, if maxAllowedClaimDivisor is set to 1, the cap becomes fund/1 = 100% — a single nuke drains the entire fund.
threats.investigation_rationale: The cap mechanism relies on consistent parameter relationships, but setNukeFactorMaxParam and setMaxAllowedClaimDivisor have no cross-validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimAmount = finalNukeFactor > nukeFactorMaxParam ? maxAllowedClaimAmount : potentialClaimAmount — no validation that potentialClaimAmount <= maxAllowedClaimAmount in the else branch

preconditions: Owner sets nukeFactorMaxParam > MAX_DENOMINATOR / maxAllowedClaimDivisor (parameter inconsistency)
OR owner sets maxAllowedClaimDivisor to 1
Token exists with finalNukeFactor between old and new nukeFactorMaxParam

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Claim cap depends on parameter consistency between nukeFactorMaxParam and maxAllowedClaimDivisor but no enforcement exists
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to set inconsistent parameters

threats.hypothesis_description: When removeDev is called, pending rewards are calculated and stored, but the dev can still call claim() afterward since there's no weight>0 check in claim(). If the owner re-adds a dev address without the dev having claimed, both old pendingRewards and new accrual are claimable. While individually correct, the interaction between removeDev, pending claims, and addDev creates a complex state where accounting depends on claim timing relative to add/remove operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The DevFund uses a MasterChef-style reward tracking system. The removeDev/addDev cycle preserves pendingRewards but doesn't enforce claim before state changes. If the owner manipulates dev weights, the reward distribution could become inconsistent with actual fund balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeDev stores pendingRewards but does not force claim; addDev does not reset pendingRewards; claim has no weight>0 requirement

preconditions: Owner removes a dev with unclaimed rewards
Owner re-adds the same dev address
Requires owner actions (admin trust)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: removeDev: info.pendingRewards += (totalRewardDebt - info.rewardDebt) * info.weight; claim: pending = info.pendingRewards + (totalRewardDebt - info.rewardDebt) * info.weight
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner can manipulate dev weight timing to affect reward distribution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Removed dev retains claimable rewards; safeRewardTransfer caps at balance preventing insolvency

economic_questions: Can remove/re-add cycle create more claimable rewards than funded?
Does safeRewardTransfer adequately prevent over-distribution?
Is the accounting always consistent across add/remove/update cycles?
validation_requirements: Trace reward state through remove-claim-readd sequences
Verify contract balance always covers sum of all pending rewards
Test edge cases with multiple devs and rapid weight changes

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Full state trace through add/remove/claim permutations

threats.title: Protocol-Wide Denial of Service via Admin Tax Cut Misconfiguration
threats.hypothesis_description: If the admin sets the taxCut parameter to 0, all core protocol functions (minting, trading, forging) will permanently revert due to division-by-zero errors.
threats.investigation_rationale: Multiple contracts (NukeFund, EntityForging, EntityTrading) use `taxCut` as a divisor to calculate fee splits (e.g., `msg.value / taxCut`). The `setTaxCut` admin functions do not enforce a minimum value of 1. If an admin accidentally or maliciously sets `taxCut` to 0, every transaction that processes fees will revert, bricking the entire protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing require(_taxCut > 0) in setter, leading to msg.value / taxCut reverting

preconditions: Admin calls setTaxCut(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing zero-check on a state variable used as a divisor

operational_questions: How are admin keys secured?
Is there a UI validation preventing the admin from inputting 0?
validation_requirements: Verify that setting taxCut to 0 causes minting to revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin key is compromised or admin makes a typo setting taxCut to 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User attempts to mint, trade, or forge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reaches fee calculation and attempts division by 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts, halting all economic activity on the protocol

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of all revenue-generating protocol functions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Easy (admin sets it back to >0), but causes severe temporary disruption

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setTaxCut(0) transaction followed by massive revert rates
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drop in protocol activity to zero

threats.title: Owner-Configurable taxCut Without Bounds Can Brick Core Flows via Division-by-Zero or Extreme Fee Skews
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple contracts use taxCut as a divisor (NukeFund.receive, EntityForging.forgeWithListed devFee calculation, EntityTrading.buyNFT contribution calculation) but setters do not enforce taxCut > 0. Setting taxCut to 0 causes immediate division-by-zero reverts, bricking mint distribution, forging fee splits, and marketplace buys. Even non-zero extreme values can materially alter economics (e.g., taxCut=1 makes 100% go to devShare/contribution path).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operationally common failure mode: a single mis-set parameter halts production. Even though it requires owner action (low realism under threat model), it is a realistic ops risk worth validating with runbooks/guards.
deployment_assumptions: Owner can accidentally or maliciously call setTaxCut(0) or extreme values
No off-chain governance controls prevent unsafe parameter changes
threats.config_evidence: NukeFund.sol:setTaxCut(uint256), EntityForging.sol:setTaxCut(uint256), EntityTrading.sol:setTaxCut(uint256) have no require(_taxCut>0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: devShare = msg.value / taxCut; no guard that taxCut>0; setter lacks require

preconditions: Owner submits a transaction setting taxCut to 0 (or unsafe extreme)
Core flows continue to route ETH to NukeFund / calculate fee splits

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical divisor parameter lacks validation across multiple contracts

operational_questions: Are there operational controls (multisig policy, CI checks, change management) preventing unsafe taxCut updates?
Is taxCut expected to be fixed at 10 permanently (design intent)?
How would the team recover given pause/unpause is missing?
validation_requirements: Parameter-bound tests: taxCut=0 and taxCut=1 effects across mint/forge/trade
Operational review: owner key management and change approval process
Add monitoring for setTaxCut events and immediate revert-rate spikes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All mints/forges/trades that send ETH to NukeFund begin reverting
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol effectively halts without an emergency pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Owner must restore taxCut to non-zero and communicate incident

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Full protocol DoS for revenue flows and marketplace/forging
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; simple parameter fix but no pause and user trust impact
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Minting fund distribution, forging fee split, trading fee split

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setTaxCut events; spike in reverted receive() calls at NukeFund
